feedback.text: When talking about software, it's important to protect the rights of consumers who obtain a license to a program that can work offline perfectly well but somehow requires constant internet activity to function. Once the company decides to stop supporting the software or it goes out of business, the customers are left with a broken program through no fault of their own. When talking about videogames in particular, there are many examples of games that could be played without relying on an internet connection that instead are artificially made to stop working by the publisher's end of support. Similarly, many games that actually require online connectivity could be made to work though the use of privately operated servers, so once the publisher drops support, the users can still set up their own version of the service and keep playing amongst themselves. I ask the commission to take into consideration and discuss this matter in the context of the Digital Fairness Act
